topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The rejection email landed in Maya’s inbox like a physical blow. She stared at the screen, the bright white text blurring slightly. Her fingers hovered over the keyboard, trembling just enough to make the keys click with exaggerated force. *Denied*. The word echoed in the quiet dorm room, hollow and sharp. She slowly pushed her chair back, the scrape of metal against the linoleum sounding deafening in the silence. She walked to the window, pulling the cheap blinds aside to look at the indifferent sprawl of the campus grounds. Every well-manicured lawn, every gleaming academic building seemed to mock her effort. A tight knot tightened in her chest, a hot, prickly sensation that made her jaw clench. She didn't bother to reread the rejection letter; the words were already etched into her memory, a definitive stamp of failure.

[story 2]

"Well, isn't that just perfect," Leo muttered, tossing the crumpled application packet onto his desk. He didn't look up when Sarah finally spoke. "Just what we needed. Another rejection." Sarah leaned back in her chair, crossing her arms over her chest, a subtle shift in her posture that spoke volumes about her displeasure. Her voice was dangerously level, devoid of warmth. "It's amazing how much effort you can pour into something, only to have it dismissed so easily." Leo felt a flush creep up his neck, a familiar heat of indignation rising in his throat. He picked up a pen, turning it over and over until the plastic felt slick under his fingers. He let the silence stretch, letting his stillness communicate the depth of his disappointment, a silent judgment aimed squarely at the empty air between them.

[story 3]

He reread the official notification three times, each time the same wave of bitter disappointment washed over him. The carefully crafted narrative he had spent weeks perfecting—the anecdotes, the quantified achievements, the sheer passion he poured into the essay—had been deemed insufficient. He slammed the laptop shut. The sound cracked through the small apartment, a sudden, jarring punctuation mark to his defeat. He stood up abruptly, pacing the cramped space. His breath came in shallow, quick gasps. He ran a hand roughly through his hair, feeling the stubble scratch against his palm. He didn't bother to check his phone; there was nothing left to see that wasn't a confirmation of the letdown. The air felt thick, heavy with unspent frustration.
